chunk_summary: Adolescent anemia evaluation focuses on iron deficiency risks, targeted screening, therapeutic dosing, adherence strategies, and program linkage for prevention and treatment.
learning_objectives: Understand the risk factors for iron deficiency anemia in adolescents, including dietary and physiological contributors.
Learn how to conduct targeted screening for anemia symptoms and underlying causes such as heavy menstrual bleeding and chronic inflammation.
Develop strategies to improve adherence to iron supplementation regimens and address common barriers.
Recognize the importance of transitioning from therapeutic dosing to preventive IFA regimens after anemia correction.
key_facts: Adolescents are at high risk for iron deficiency due to growth spurts, dietary restrictions, and menstruation-related blood loss.
Weekly Iron and Folic Acid Supplementation (WIFS) programs are implemented through schools or community platforms but adherence varies.
Screening should include CBC morphology, dietary behaviors, and symptoms of heavy menstrual bleeding (e.g., changing pads every 1–2 hours).
Therapeutic iron dosing should be linked to daily routines and adjusted for tolerability (e.g., nausea on an empty stomach).
Incomplete hemoglobin response may indicate hemoglobinopathy, chronic disease, or combined micronutrient deficiencies.
reference_quote: Programmatic weekly IFA supplementation through school or community platforms may already be occurring, but adherence is variable and regimens differ by implementation site. (Source: National Health Mission, WIFS operational guidelines)
document_key_topics: Screening and diagnostic strategies for anemia
